review.summary: The series adds a useful tuning knob, but the RDMA chunk size becomes part of the migration wire interpretation without any source/destination negotiation. A mismatched setting between peers will make the receiver decode chunk indices with a different granularity and can register or write the wrong host range. There is also a secondary validation gap where the new machine property path can accept invalid values that are only caught later by an assert.

stages.A: API/QAPI wiring is mostly complete: QAPI, HMP set/info, defaults, and apply paths were updated consistently.
stages.B: Core RDMA logic is not safe yet because chunk size now changes address/chunk mapping semantics used on both ends of the protocol.
stages.C: Backward/operational compatibility is incomplete: the new parameter must be negotiated or otherwise enforced symmetrically across source and destination.
stages.D: Validation is incomplete because only migrate_params_check() validates the new range/power-of-two constraint; the property setter path still accepts unchecked values.
stages.E: There are checkpatch spacing errors around shift expressions, but those are cosmetic compared to the protocol issue.

findings.title: Chunk-size is used as a local-only knob even though it changes RDMA wire interpretation
findings.description: The patch replaces the fixed 1 MiB chunking with migrate_rdma_chunk_size() in both chunk index generation and chunk index decoding. That means the numeric chunk identifier carried in RDMA registration state is no longer self-describing: the sender computes reg->key.chunk using its local x-rdma-chunk-size, while the receiver reconstructs host_addr using its own local x-rdma-chunk-size. If the two QEMU processes are configured differently, the destination will resolve the same chunk number to a different GPA/HVA range, which can corrupt registration bookkeeping or cause writes/invalidations against the wrong memory range. The series does not add protocol negotiation, versioning, or an explicit compatibility check to ensure both peers use the same chunk size.
findings.suggestion: Make the chunk size a negotiated migration parameter that is exchanged and fixed for both ends before any RDMA registration data is interpreted, or keep the wire-visible chunking constant and use the new knob only for local batching/flush behavior. At minimum, reject migration startup if source and destination chunk sizes differ.
findings.confidence_reason: The same on-wire chunk number is now encoded and decoded with a runtime value taken independently from each side, and the patch shows no synchronization path for that value.

findings.title: Invalid x-rdma-chunk-size values can still enter through the property path
findings.description: The new parameter is validated in migrate_params_check() for QMP/HMP updates, but the default/property registration uses DEFINE_PROP_UINT64 directly. That setter does not enforce the documented power-of-two and 1M..1024M range. A user can therefore inject an invalid value through machine/global properties, and the failure is deferred until RDMA code calls migrate_rdma_chunk_size(), where it hits an assert instead of returning a user-facing configuration error.
findings.suggestion: Add property-level validation, for example via a custom setter/check hook, so all configuration entry points enforce the same constraints and report a normal error before RDMA migration starts.
findings.confidence_reason: QEMU migration parameters commonly have multiple entry paths; the patch only shows explicit validation in migrate_params_check() while the property definition remains a plain uint64 setter and the runtime accessor relies on assert().

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
